claim: Kernel fusion can recover 30% of wasted inference performance by eliminating unnecessary kernel launches overhead.
actionable: When optimizing local LLM inference, profile kernel launches for inefficiencies and fuse separate operations to reduce launch overhead.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: RDMA integration can reduce inter-node communication latency from 300 microseconds to single digits, enabling efficient tensor parallelism across multiple devices.
actionable: Use RDMA for distributed inference to minimize synchronization overhead when splitting models across connected devices.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Prefill phase is compute-bound and less important than decode for local inference, especially when running single-user workloads.
actionable: Focus local inference optimization on the decode phase (memory-bound) rather than prefill; prefill accounts for smaller impact on latency in single-user scenarios.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: For inference, memory bandwidth and capacity are more important than raw compute; inference is memory-bound unlike training which is compute-bound.
actionable: When selecting hardware for local inference, prioritize memory bandwidth and capacity over floating point operations; allocate budget toward memory optimization.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Decoding phase success depends on three factors: (1) fitting model into memory, (2) sufficient memory bandwidth, (3) energy per byte efficiency.
actionable: Assess local inference feasibility using: available memory capacity, memory bandwidth (GB/s), and power consumption per byte moved.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Intelligence per joule metric improves 5x from hardware improvements and 3x from model improvements, compounding to roughly 15x overall progress in 2 years.
actionable: Track intelligence per joule as a unified metric to measure improvements across hardware, software, and models for local inference.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: A 100x improvement is theoretically achievable in local inference performance through full-stack optimization: kernels, orchestration, models, and hardware co-design.
actionable: Approach local frontier AI performance gains holistically; improvements compound across kernel fusion, network latency, model architecture, and specialized hardware.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Phones have fundamental energy constraints limiting local inference: ~10-15 watt-hours battery vs 10-15 watts power draw equals ~1 hour usable runtime.
actionable: Avoid on-phone LLM inference for extended use until energy consumption per inference drops significantly or battery density improves.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Splitting prefill and decode across heterogeneous hardware (e.g., RTX 5090 for prefill, Mac for decode) provides 2x speedup on large prompts with 10 gigabit Ethernet.
actionable: For large prompts, split inference: run compute-intensive prefill on GPUs with high FLOPS, stream KV cache via 10Gbps network, run memory-bound decode on high-bandwidth hardware.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Most consumer use cases will saturate in intelligence at a threshold where larger models provide diminishing returns (e.g., transcription, summarization).
actionable: Profile intelligence curves for your application; identify the minimum model size that meets acceptable quality, then optimize for speed/efficiency rather than scale.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Hardware lottery: GPU-centric research assumes training-optimized hardware, but inference on consumer hardware (Mac with unified memory) has unexplored optimization potential.
actionable: Research inference optimizations on actual target hardware architectures rather than assuming GPU-training paradigms apply; consumer hardware may have unique efficiency opportunities.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Harness matters significantly for inference performance: identical models produce different speeds across frameworks due to kernel scheduling and memory management.
actionable: Benchmark inference across different frameworks (MLX, llama.cpp, etc.) for the same model; harness efficiency can swing performance by 2x+ on resource-constrained hardware.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: One-bit quantization and heavy pruning without understanding degradation are cargo-cult optimizations; smaller unoptimized models often outperform heavily quantized large models.
actionable: Avoid blind quantization; compare a 0.6B unquantized model against a 7B one-bit model on your benchmark before choosing; measure quality impact, not just token/sec.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Full-stack co-design (models, hardware, software) is necessary for frontier performance; specialized chips (Talos, Groq) make sense once model architectures stabilize.
actionable: If building systems for stable workloads, justify custom silicon investment by: benchmarking cloud-equivalent cost over 3-5 years and ensuring model API stability.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Test-time scaling (best-of-N sampling, search-based inference) enables smaller models to match larger models through increased compute, enabling local batching.
actionable: For latency-tolerant workloads, run multiple inference passes via search/verification rather than a single large model; batch these passes locally for efficiency.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Test-time training (updating model weights at inference) breaks cloud batching economics by making each user request a unique model; favors local inference.
actionable: Monitor emerging test-time training research; if adopted widely, local inference gains 10x relative advantage over cloud due to inability to batch personalized models.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Mesh networking (EXO) with automatic hardware discovery enables zero-configuration distributed inference across heterogeneous devices without manual networking setup.
actionable: Use EXO or similar mesh frameworks to automatically cluster consumer hardware; reduces setup friction compared to manual distributed inference orchestration.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Event sourcing architecture for distributed inference maintains consistency across dynamic clusters where devices can power off, sleep, or rejoin.
actionable: If building distributed local inference, use append-only logs to tolerate device churn; replicate cluster state across nodes to survive node failure mid-inference.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Mac with 512GB memory and RTX 5090 have inverse tradeoffs: Mac has 10x more memory but half the bandwidth; RTX has 10x less memory but higher compute.
actionable: When combining Mac + RTX: use RTX for prefill (compute-heavy, small output), Mac for decode (memory-bound); this reverses typical GPU-dominance pattern.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs

claim: Within 18 months to 2 years, $5,000 local hardware should achieve near-frontier model performance through hardware commoditization and full-stack optimization.
actionable: Consider local inference investment if amortized token costs exceed $1/100K tokens annually; breakeven improving as hardware prices drop.
source_title: Run Frontier AI at Home — Alex Cheema, EXO Labs
